retrieved_docs.title: Cerebral Autosomal Dominant Arteriopathy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: CADASIL should be suspected in patients with a strong family history of early strokes and dementia, keeping in mind that CADASIL is likely underdiagnosed. Though definitive diagnosis is via genetic testing or skin biopsy, a CADASIL scale has been proposed by Pescini et al. as a less expensive initial clinical screening measure. [13] This scale includes typical symptoms of the disease, such as migraines and TIA, typical imaging features, and family history, all with various weightings. In patients where CADASIL is strongly suspected, definitive diagnosis begins with serum genetic testing for a NOTCH3 mutation. Approximately 4% of patients with CADASIL will have a negative genetic test, likely related to unidentified genetic mutations. Thus, for those patients with a high clinical suspicion, the next step is a skin biopsy with histopathologic examination for granular osmiophilic material (GOM) accumulation. [14] Skin biopsy has a specificity of 100% and sensitivity of 45% to 100% due to variations in disease progression and focality of the disease process.

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslexia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Dyslexia is hereditary; however, home literacy and the teaching quality strongly impact the child’s reading ability. Twin studies have shown that reading difficulties are highly heritable, and recent genetic studies have found several small genes that affect reading development. [2] These genes affect neuron migration, cortical morphogenesis, the outgrowth of neurites, and the structure and function of cilia. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Nucleus Caudate -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Polymorphisms in the DRD2.ANKK1 Taq1A gene can also influence caudate nucleus volume: those with the A allele have smaller caudate nucleus volumes at advanced ages than non-carriers, which are not detectable before old age. [24] Differences in nucleotides near the FAT3 genecan also influence the volume of the caudate nucleus in nucleotides near the FAT3 gene, which encodes a cadherin utilized during neurodevelopment.

retrieved_docs.title: Schizotypal Personality Disorder -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Some common chromosome regions are shared by schizotypal personality disorder and schizophrenia. [6] Among possible candidate genes, the dystrobrevin-binding protein 1 (DTNBP1) gene located on chromosome 6q22.3, which codes for a synaptic protein dysbindin 1, has been associated with schizophrenia and paranoid schizotypy. Functional alterations in the catechol- O -methyltransferase gene (COMT) in the form of a single-nucleotide polymorphism on codon 158, which codes for an enzyme involved in the breakdown of dopamine at the synapse, are believed to contribute to negative and cognitive symptoms in schizophrenia and higher schizotypy scores. [6] According to an older study, platelet monoamine oxidase levels are associated with sociability levels, with low levels occurring in schizotypal personality disorder. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Targeting and I Cell Diseases -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, Okada et al reported several cases of exceptional levels of cognitive development where children were independent, toilet-trained, had good academic performance, could sing songs, and recognized foreign words. Autopsy studies on patients with ICD have not revealed any brain abnormalities. [76] [78] This finding is further supported by studies on knock-in mice, which have demonstrated that active Niemann-Pick C2 protein maintains cellular functioning and prevents neurodegeneration in ICD. Cathepsin D and B also remain unaffected in ICD since they use M6P-independent pathways to transport neuronal lysosomes. [33] Taber et al suggest that hepatomegaly is a feature mainly identified with Hurler syndrome. However, other reports have identified liver enlargement in patients with ICD. [68] [78]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Complement Cascade -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Beyond its immune-related functions, complement activity also links to non-immune-related pathways, such as development and tissue homeostasis. During normal development, C3a mediates the mutual cell attraction that is necessary for collective cell migration in Xenopus laevis, and collectin 11 (also known as CLK1) of the lectin complement pathway regulates the migration of neural crest cells. [13] Other studies suggest a role for complement in stem cell or progenitor cell fate decisions. It has been shown that CD46 expression induced by the Notch ligand Delta-like ligand 1 (DLL1) is crucial for human epidermal progenitor cell proliferation and self-renewal and that anaphylatoxins support the maintenance of the pluripotent state of human embryonic stem cells, mediate the mobilization of hematopoietic stem cells from the bone marrow, and promote the migration and proliferation of cardiac pluripotent progenitor cells. [14] [15] [16] C3a and C5a receptors also facilitate osteoclast differentiation and bone formation. [17] At the level of whole organs, C1q is required for normal neuron maturation as it directs the elimination of excitatory synapses in the brain via ‘synapse pruning,’ and it can also protect neurons against fibrillar amyloid beta-mediated injury through the induction of phosphorylated cAMP-response element-binding protein and activator protein 1 (AP-1), which are transcription factors that are associated with neuronal survival and neurite outgrowth. [18] [19] By contrast, increased C1q levels correlate with a decline in synaptic plasticity, cognitive function, and tissue regeneration during aging by directly increasing the induction of WNT pathways [20] , which suggests that targeting C1q could be a potential approach for the treatment of dementia. The designated form of C3a (C3a-desArg; also known as acylation-stimulating protein) and, as shown more recently, adipocytes and stimulate triglyceride synthesis in adipocytes to produce C3a, and systemic complement has an important homeostatic and regulatory role in metabolic organs such as the pancreas, liver, and adipose tissue. The recently discovered link between intracellular C3aR-mediated signals and the mTOR network suggests that complement may also participate in metabolic sensing. mTOR integrates signals from pathways that sense cellular nutrients, oxygen, and energy levels and translates these signals into appropriate responses, such as apoptosis induction, inhibition of autophagy, and/or cell activation and proliferation. Recent research found that CD46 controls the expression of amino acid and glucose transporters and the assembly of mTOR complex 1 (mTORC1) in activated CD4+ T cells. Furthermore, C3a regulates proteasome activity and, thereby, normal protein turnover in human retinal epithelial cells88. This relationship suggests that the connection between complement and metabolism may extend to sensing nutrient and/or cellular stress and subsequent modulation of catabolic and anabolic metabolism at the single-cell level.

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebral Autosomal Dominant Arteriopathy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Originally, CADASIL was recognized as a heritable early-onset microvascular disease of unknown etiology. The genetic cause of CADASIL was identified by Joutel et al in 1996 as multiple mutations involving genes encoding for the NOTCH3 protein on chromosome 19. CADASIL is the most common inherited small vessel disease caused by mutations involving a single gene. This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion. [2] Even within families, there is significant variability in presentation. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hereditary Endothelial Dystrophy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In 2016, a novel variant of CHED was reported in a 45-year-old woman that resulted in delayed onset of the disease. She reported poor bilateral vision since childhood and rapid decline of vision the past 5-years. CHED was confirmed by slit-lamp examination, histopathology, genetic screening, and clinical correlation. Genetic testing confirmed new compound heterozygous mutations affecting the SLC4A11 gene. [15] Due to this single and unusual presentation, it is difficult to interpret the reason for a delayed onset. It has been suggested that the woman’s lack of access to care and socioeconomic status contributed to her delay in seeking care, but it has also been considered that compound heterozygous mutations of SLC4A11 may only mildly affect BTR function, resulting in delayed onset of CHED signs and symptoms. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Fahr Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- (D) Genetic Counseling
retrieved_docs.content: There are no demonstrated medical benefits for screening the relatives. Predictive testing in the form of brain CT scan (calcium deposits precede clinical manifestations by years) and molecular testing (if PDGFB, PDGFRB, XPR1, SLC20A2 pathogenic variant has been identified in proband) can be offered to asymptomatic first-degree relatives (>18 years) after weighing the ethical and psychosocial conditions because no curative treatment is available. Testing individuals >18 years can help them in decision making in regards to their marriage, family planning, career, and finance. Testing is usually not recommended in individuals <18 years considering social issues, discrimination and anxiety in future, negative impact on parent-child or sibling-child relationship, and uncertain CT scan predictability as penetrance is age-dependent.

retrieved_docs.title: Depressive Cognitive Disorders  -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic factors: C9ORF72 repeats on chromosome 9 are found in patients experiencing depressive cognitive disorders. This has been earlier correlated with neurodegenerative dementia; this finding confirms the genetic association of depressive cognitive disorders. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebral Autosomal Dominant Arteriopathy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral autosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (CADASIL) is an inherited disease primarily manifesting with migraines and premature small vessel ischemic disease. Characteristic magnetic resonance imaging findings usually emerge by age 30, aiding diagnosis via genetic testing or skin biopsy. With no disease-modifying therapies, treatment focuses on cardiovascular risk reduction, with caution in antiplatelet agent use due to bleeding tendencies. Typically, disability arises by age 58.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic Hearing Loss -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic SNHL can have significant social, economic, and medical repercussions. Prognosis is dependent on the etiology and can be improved with early diagnosis and intervention. [39] The longer the time from diagnosis to intervention, the poorer the speech and language outcomes will become, as well as poorer cognitive development. [40] Simultaneous exposure to more than one spoken language in the home is also associated with worse outcomes. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Cognition: cognitive impairment is one of the most common and challenging manifestations of childhood DM1. CDM patients are most affected, with IQ range 40 to 80, mean 70 (average normal 100). Cognitive impairment correlates with the severity of weakness, size of CTG repeat, and maternal transmission.

retrieved_docs.title: Lacunar Stroke -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The development of small vessel disease, causing a lacunar stroke, is mainly due to underlying medical conditions such as hypertension and diabetes mellitus. Other risk factors include smoking, LDL levels, carotid artery atherosclerosis, peripheral artery disease, previous TIA, and hyperhomocysteinemia. [4] Genetic factors also increase the risk of developing small vessel disease, leading to lacunar infarcts. Cerebral autosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (CADASIL) is a rare genetic disorder that can cause small vessel arteriopathy. Caused by mutations in the NOTCH3 gene on chromosome 19 , CADASIL is the most common inherited cause of stroke and dementia in adults. [9] While clinical presentations vary, the most common symptoms and signs, starting around the third or fourth decade of life, include the following: recurrent ischemic strokes, transient ischemic attacks, progressive white matter degeneration, memory loss, migraine with aura, debilitating dementia, and multiple psychiatric symptoms. [10] A pathognomic feature of CADASIL is granular osmophilic material found near blood vessels and can be seen on skin biopsy under electron microscopy. The result is degeneration of blood vessels, and the disease is incurable and fatal. Characteristic magnetic resonance imaging features are white matter hyperintensities in the anterior temporal lobes, external capsules, and superior frontal gyri. [9] Other less common genes associated with cerebral small vessel disease are HTRA1, TREX1, GLA, COL4A1, COL4A2, FOXC1 , and PITX2 . The ApoE4 gene may also confer increased susceptibility to cerebral small vessel disease, in addition to being a risk factor for Alzheimer disease. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cerebral Autoregulation -- Mechanism -- Myogenic Tone
retrieved_docs.content: Smooth muscle cell myogenic regulation is important in cerebral autosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (CADASIL). Patients with CADASIL show a degree of smooth muscle cell degeneration in small cerebral arteries. Studies have demonstrated impaired myogenic autoregulatory functioning in animal models and individuals with the genetic condition. [8] [9] [10] This disease results from a mutation in the NOTCH3 gene and is marked by recurrent ischemic strokes, cognitive impairment, subcortical dementia, mood disturbances like depression and apathy, as well as premature death. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Myotonic Dystrophy -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Sleep: excessive sleep disorder and sleep apnea may adversely affect learning, memory, high-level cognitive processing, and physical functioning, exacerbating psychomotor and cognitive delays.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 930
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy, type 1 is a hereditary cerebrovascular disorder that characteristically presents with a gait disorder (اضطراب المِشْيَة). The disease’s underlying vascular pathology leads to subcortical infarctions and diffuse white‑matter changes, which together disrupt motor pathways and result in abnormal walking patterns. Recognizing gait disturbance as a key manifestation of this specific type of cerebral arteriopathy aids clinicians in diagnosing the condition and guiding appropriate genetic counseling and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: sequelae (dementia and related neurodegenerative conditions), interventional neurosurgery (various ventricular shunts), multimodal diagnostics (head CT, EEG and ocular motor monitoring), and the genetic‑hereditary context of diffuse encephalitis within the SMQ disease family, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and management.

Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.

An eye‑related subtype, “الشقيقة الشالة للعين,” is recorded as a child of شقيقة and as a descendant of the cranial‑nerve disorder “اضطراب بالعصب القحفي,” reflecting a specific manifestation that involves ocular symptoms. Genetic and hereditary forms are captured by “Hereditary disorder of nervous system,” which is inversely related to the familial migraine entity “شقيقة فالجية عائلية.” These two migraine variants are noted as clinically similar, underscoring their shared pathophysiology despite possible differences in inheritance patterns. In the context of cerebrovascular disease, the rare condition “CEREBRAL ARTERIOPATHY, AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT, WITH SUBCORTICAL INFARCTS AND LEUKOENCEPHALOPATHY, TYPE 1” lists شقيقة as one of its manifestations, linking migraine to subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of brain and chest and Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are both primary mappings of the computerized axial tomography (CT) scan head. The CT head examination is clinically associated with خرف (dementia) and commonly co‑occurs with خرف شيخوخي (senile dementia), indicating its relevance in the evaluation and management of cognitive decline, particularly age‑related dementia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine (شقيقة) is represented in the knowledge base as a specific nervous‑system disorder that resides within a hierarchy of headache and neurological conditions, being a child of broader entities such as “Headache; including migraine,” “Migraine and Headache Syndromes,” and the general category “اضطراب الجهاز العصبي” (nervous‑system disorder). It is also classified as a type of vascular headache (صداع وعائي) and is closely related to, and sometimes synonymous with, that category. Familial hemiplegic migraine (شقيقة فالجية عائلية) is recorded as a hereditary form of the disorder, linking migraine to genetic and hereditary nervous‑system diseases. Clinically, migraine may appear as a manifestation of other neurological diseases, for example in cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant, with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy (type 1), where it co‑occurs with gait disturbance. Patient histories noting “H/O: migraine” are mapped to personal histories of nervous‑system and

This unified structure supports coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of a broad array of neurological conditions, emphasizing the central role of peripheral nerves, pain, paralysis, and targeted surgical decompression in clinical practice.

Cerebral arteriopathy, autosomal dominant with subcortical infarcts and leukoencephalopathy type 1, illustrates the overlap between vascular cerebral disease, gait disorder, and migraine, emphasizing the interconnected nature of vascular, headache, and neurodegenerative processes. Overall, the community’s knowledge highlights the anatomical sites (brain, ventricles, nervous system), clinical associations (dementia, ischemic complications, cranial nerve dysfunction, gait disturbance), genetic and familial forms, and the role of neurosurgical procedures (extracranial ventricular shunting, nasopharyngeal diversion) and pharmacologic prevention (sodium valproate) in integrated diagnosis and management of these interrelated neurological conditions.
